figures.gloss: An enigmatic infantile deity with a downturned, snarling mouth, toothless gums, and a deeply cleft head, often depicted weeping. Rendered in exquisite jade or held in the arms of rulers, it is recognized as a primeval spirit of rain, thunder, and earth.
figures.facet: It captures the terrifying vulnerability of a divine infant—a crying baby whose tears are the rain itself, intimately linking human reproduction, infantile need, and the fierce predatory power of the jaguar.
figures.provenance: Peter David Joralemon, 'A Study of Olmec Iconography' (1971); Michael D. Coe, 'The Olmec World' (1996).

figures.gloss: The primary Olmec earth-deity, a chimerical apex predator blending features of the caiman, eagle, and jaguar. Recognizable by its L-shaped eyes, flaming eyebrows, and a serrated crest, it serves as the animate crust of the earth.
figures.facet: Rather than a chaotic monster to be slain, the Olmec Dragon is the necessary, living foundation of agriculture—a cosmic beast whose very body provides the fertile soil and the caves that act as sacred portals.
figures.provenance: Karl Taube, 'Olmec Art at Dumbarton Oaks' (2004).

figures.gloss: The earliest known iteration of the pan-Mesoamerican plumed serpent, famously depicted on La Venta Monument 19. It combines the undulating, earth-bound body of a rattlesnake with the crest and feathers of a raptor, sheltering a human figure in its coils.
figures.facet: It appears here not yet as the fully abstracted philosophical creator-god of later empires, but as a visceral, localized spirit of wind and flight—a literal, biological joining of the sky's apex and the earth's belly.
figures.provenance: Karl Taube, 'The Major Gods of Ancient Yucatan' (1992); Michael D. Coe, 'Mexico' (2013).

figures.gloss: Massive, individuated basalt portraits of Olmec rulers, weighing up to fifty tons and transported from distant volcanic mountains. Wearing distinct helmets likely used in the ritual ballgame or for warfare, they gaze outward with powerful, heavy-lidded expressions.
figures.facet: They physically anchor the authority of the ruler to the landscape. Stripped of bodies, the head alone—as the seat of the soul and cosmic force—acts as an immovable axis mundi for the ceremonial center.
figures.provenance: Ann Cyphers, 'Las cabezas colosales' (1996); Matthew W. Stirling, 'Discovering the New World's Oldest Dated Work of Man' (1939).

figures.gloss: A ubiquitous iconographic V-shaped notch or cleft, appearing on the heads of deities, celts, and stone altars. It represents the cosmic fracture from which maize originally sprouted and through which spiritual forces emerge from the underworld.
figures.facet: It collapses the concepts of a cranial wound, an agricultural furrow, and a geological cave into a single geometric motif, transforming the physical rupture of the earth into the literal landscape of genesis.
figures.provenance: Kent V. Reilly III, 'Art, Ritual, and Rulership in the Olmec World' (1994).

figures.gloss: A deity featuring a prominently cleft head from which an ear of corn or a vegetal motif emerges. Carved on countless greenstone celts, it represents the vital, animating force of the domesticated harvest.
figures.facet: The deity does not simply provide maize; it is the maize. The Olmec envisioned agriculture as a profoundly violent and miraculous act of birth from the fractured head of a divine being.

figures.gloss: A fearsome raptorial deity, often resembling a Harpy Eagle, heavily associated with the sky realm, rulership, and the sun. It is frequently depicted with a hooked beak, prominent talons, and cross-banded motifs indicating celestial authority.
figures.facet: It represents the sky not as a distant, peaceful canopy, but as an active, predatory force. It crowns the rulers, validating their terrestrial violence through the sheer, unapproachable dominance of the apex aerial hunter.
figures.provenance: Peter David Joralemon, 'A Study of Olmec Iconography' (1971).

figures.gloss: A terrifying, chthonic marine deity. Characterized by a crescent-shaped eye, a single prominent shark tooth, and crossed bands, it ruled the watery underworld and the abyssal depths bordering the Gulf Coast.
figures.facet: As a coastal civilization, the Olmec projected their underworld not just into terrestrial caves, but into the dark, predatory depths of the sea, defining the abyss as a realm ruled by marine predators rather than humanoid lords.
figures.provenance: Karl Taube, 'Olmec Art at Dumbarton Oaks' (2004).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The infant were-jaguar is repeatedly depicted emerging from the V-shaped cleft in the head of the Earth Dragon.
Intra-tradition bond: The maize deity is fundamentally defined by the cleft portal, literally sprouting from this cosmic wound.
Intra-tradition bond: The fundamental cosmological duality of Olmec art, contrasting the chthonic earth-caiman with the celestial sky-raptor.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Olmec weeping rain-baby is the direct iconographic and conceptual ancestor of the Aztec and Teotihuacan rain gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A documented iconographic lineage stretching from La Venta directly to the Aztec creator deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Olmec 'were-animal' motif is the foundational root of the pan-Mesoamerican belief in animal spirit companions and shamanic transformation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rulers depicted in the colossal heads wear the thick protective helmets utilized in the sacred Mesoamerican ballgame.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A monstrous, chimerical earth/sea being whose body forms the foundation of the world and generates natural wealth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The physical and ritual veneration of formidable, heavy ancestral presences that anchor and protect the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dying and rising god whose life cycle is violently and intimately bound to the sprouting and harvesting of staple crops.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A formidable sovereign of the watery abyss, commanding the terrifying and resource-rich depths of the sea.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A sovereign of the earth whose physical fracture and subsequent sprouting guarantees the rebirth of crops and society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An apex celestial raptor whose iconography is leveraged by human elites to signify divine violence and atmospheric power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, atmospheric serpent associated with severe weather, acting as a living conduit between the earth and torrential skies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial, world-shaping chimerical beast that commands water and earth, leaving physical landmarks in its wake.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sacred, localized geographic fissure through which original ancestors and vital spirits cross from the lower world into this one.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Olmec, the earliest complex society of Mesoamerica, on the Gulf Coast lowlands of Veracruz and Tabasco (Mexico), flourishing c. 1500–400 BCE at centers such as San Lorenzo and La Venta. Often called Mesoamerica's 'mother culture,' it is known only archaeologically — reconstructed from monumental basalt sculpture and jade iconography, with no surviving texts and no living heirs of the cult.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmology is inferred from a recurring cast of supernaturals identified through iconography — a were-jaguar rain spirit, an earth 'dragon' (a caiman-eagle-jaguar composite), a plumed serpent, a maize god sprouting from a cleft head, a raptorial sky-being, and a shark or fish monster of the watery depths. A layered world of sky, earth, and underworld is joined by caves and the V-shaped 'cleft,' at once a portal of emergence and the furrow from which maize is born. Rulership was fused with the sacred, kings depicted cradling the were-jaguar, though no afterlife doctrine survives.
dossier.practice: Reconstructed practice includes colossal-scale monument building and the carving of individualized ruler-portraits (the colossal heads); the deposit of enormous caches of jade and serpentine celts and of buried mosaic 'pavements' as offerings at La Venta; and votive deposits of carved wooden busts and rubber balls at the waterlogged shrine of El Manatí. The rubber ballgame, ritual bloodletting, and shamanic were-animal transformation are all indicated in the imagery.
dossier.canon: None legible as texts: the possibly proto-glyphic Cascajal Block is undeciphered, so the 'canon' is the corpus of monuments, thrones, stelae, and portable jades that carry the iconography.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the birth of a religious iconography — the first fixing in stone of a symbolic vocabulary of the sacred (were-jaguar, plumed serpent, cleft-emergence) that later Mesoamerican civilizations would inherit.
sources: Peter David Joralemon, 'A Study of Olmec Iconography' (1971)
Karl A. Taube, 'Olmec Art at Dumbarton Oaks' (2004)
Richard A. Diehl, 'The Olmecs: America's First Civilization' (2004)
Michael D. Coe & Rex Koontz, 'Mexico: From the Olmecs to the Aztecs' (2013)
